sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=10, col=31), MOUSE(row=34, col=10).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 29, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: N=14, Y=23 (both max) still doesn't complete. All 4 size combos tested. Container shapes have different hashes (different orientations). Crosses are broken.
- Goal model: Maybe need to click red dots at specific shape sizes, or interact with container shape orientations.
- Action model: Container shapes toggle sizes. Need to test clicking red dots at different sizes.
- Plan: Check red dot positions and try clicking them when shapes are at max size.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 28 actions; a strong score needs about 20 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see 10 red pixels and 2 black pixels. The red pixels include:
- Cross at (10, 52) with arms at (9,52), (10,51), (10,53), (11,52) - but center is BLACK at (10, 52)
- Cross at (52, 10) with arms at (51,10), (52,9), (52,11), (53,10) - but center is BLACK at (52, 10)
- Red dot in N-shape at (10, 31)
- Red dot in Y-bar at (34, 10)
Wait, the cross centers are at (10, 52) and (52, 10), and they're BLACK. The cross ARMS are still red. So the crosses are "broken" (centers turned black).
When I clicked the red dots inside the shapes, it only changed the timer (board_changed=True but level_completed=False).
I'm really stuck. Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Clicking on the green bars (external) - only affects timer
2. Clicking on the green bars (container) - only affects timer
3. Clicking on the container frames - only affects timer
4. Clicking on the container N/Y shapes - toggles external sizes
5. Clicking on the red dots - only affects timer
6. Clicking on the cross arms - haven't tried this!
7. Clicking on the cross centers (black) - only affects timer
8. Clicking on the external shapes themselves - haven't tried clicking on the N/Y pixels (not the red dots)
9. Clicking on the background - haven't tried
Let me try clicking on the cross ARMS (the red pixels around the black centers). Maybe this restores the crosses or does something else.
The cross arms are at:
- Top-right cross: (9, 52), (10, 51), (10, 53), (11, 52)
- Bottom-left cross: (51, 10), (52, 9), (52, 11), (53, 10)
Let me try clicking on one of the cross arms.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: All 4 size combos tested, clicking red dots only affects timer. Crosses have black centers with red arms. Haven't tried clicking cross ARMS.
Goal model: Maybe clicking cross arms restores crosses or triggers something.
Action model: Need to test clicking on cross arm pixels.
Plan: Try clicking on red cross arms to see if they restore the crosses or do something meaningful.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on cross arms (red pixels around black centers)
# Top-right cross arms: (9,52), (10,51), (10,53), (11,52)
# Bottom-left cross arms: (51,10), (52,9), (52,11), (53,10)

# Click top-right cross arm at (9, 52)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 9, 'col': 52}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
# Check if crosses changed
r_count = sum(1 for n in seg if n['color'] == 'r' and n['pixels'] == 1)
b_count = sum(1 for n in seg if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 1)
print(f"After clicking cross arm: r_count={r_count}, b_count={b_count}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

# If that didn't work, try clicking on the external N-shape itself (not the red dot)
# The N-shape is at rows 9-11, cols 28-32
# Let's click on a pixel that's part of the N-shape but not the red dot
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 9, 'col': 28}])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
n_sizes = [n['pixels'] for n in seg2 if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] >= 5]
print(f"After clicking N-shape: N sizes={n_sizes}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

# Try clicking on the external yellow bar itself
result3 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 9}])
seg3 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
y_sizes = [n['pixels'] for n in seg3 if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] >= 14]
print(f"After clicking Y-bar: Y sizes={y_sizes}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After clicking cross arm: r_count=10, b_count=2, level_completed=False
After clicking N-shape: N sizes=[14, 7, 7], level_completed=False
After clicking Y-bar: Y sizes=[23], level_completed=False
